content.text: Every action a coding agent takes ultimately produces or modifies source code. Code generation is the process by which an agent translates a task description — and the structured knowledge it has assembled about the surrounding codebase — into syntactically correct, semantically sound, and stylistically consistent code. Understanding this process in depth is what separates agents that produce usable output from those that generate plausible-looking text that fails on the first compile.

attrs.alt: Code generation pipeline: task request flows through context retrieval, prompt assembly, LLM generation, static validation, and test execution to produce generated code; a codebase knowledge store feeds the retrieval step; validation errors trigger iterative retry

content.text: What It Is

Code generation, in the context of a coding agent, is the act of producing new source code from a high-level intent. The output may be a new function, a complete file, a patch against existing code, a boilerplate scaffold, a test suite, or a migration script. What distinguishes agent-driven code generation from simple autocomplete is the multi-step pipeline that surrounds the LLM call: context retrieval, prompt construction, output validation, and iterative refinement.

The LLM itself is only one component. Before the model generates a single token, the agent must have assembled a prompt that includes the exact function signatures the new code will call, the types it must produce, the patterns already in use in the file being edited, and enough few-shot examples to anchor the style. After the model completes, the agent must verify that the output parses, type-checks, and behaves correctly before committing it. The full loop — retrieve, assemble, generate, validate — is what makes code generation reliable rather than merely impressive.

Code generation tasks range from small additions (a single method inside an existing class) to large-scale synthesis (an entire REST API layer derived from a schema definition). The techniques for reliable generation scale across this range: the same prompt-engineering and validation patterns apply at every size, though the context window demands and the number of validation passes both increase with the size of the output.
Why It Matters

Code generation is the primary value-delivery mechanism of a coding agent. Navigation and understanding are prerequisites; editing and diffing are refinement operations; but generation is the step that actually creates new functionality. A coding agent that cannot generate correct, runnable code is not useful regardless of how well it navigates the codebase or understands its structure.

The practical impact of reliable code generation is significant. Tasks that previously required an experienced engineer to spend an afternoon — writing a test suite for an untested module, implementing a new API endpoint consistent with an existing set, refactoring a class to conform to an updated interface — can be completed in minutes when the agent's generation pipeline is robust. The bottleneck shifts from writing code to reviewing it, which is a more efficient use of engineer time.

Code generation also forces the entire pipeline to become more disciplined. An agent that generates code must have an accurate model of the types it calls, the interfaces it implements, and the invariants it must maintain. This need for accuracy drives better code understanding, better context retrieval, and better validation — improvements that benefit all other agent operations as well.
Benefits
Velocity for repetitive patterns.

Generating CRUD handlers, test cases, serializers, and migration scripts from a schema is mechanical work. An agent with a good generation pipeline does this work accurately at a speed no human can match.
Consistency across a codebase.

When the agent grounds generation in real examples from the codebase, new code inherits the naming conventions, error handling patterns, logging idioms, and import style already in use — without the agent needing to be explicitly told any of those conventions.
Correctness under a strict validation loop.

Because the agent can run the compiler, type checker, and test suite, generation errors are caught immediately and fed back into the next generation attempt. The final output has passed automated checks that would take a human comparable time to run.
Reduced cognitive load on engineers.

Engineers move from writing boilerplate to reviewing diffs. Review is a higher-leverage activity: catching logical errors, security issues, and design problems that are genuinely hard for the agent but easy for a human who knows the system.
Cross-language portability.
The same agent pipeline can generate code in Python, TypeScript, Go, or Rust. The LLM's multi-language knowledge is available to every project, without the agent needing separate logic per language.
Trade-offs
Context window constraints.

Every token of context used for retrieval and examples is unavailable for the generated output. Generating a 500-line file while including sufficient context for correctness can be infeasible in a single call, requiring splitting the task or accepting reduced context quality.
Hallucination risk for novel APIs.

If the codebase uses a library version the LLM was not trained on, or an internal library with no public documentation, the model may generate calls to methods that do not exist. Validation via type checking catches some of this, but not all.
Correctness of logic, not just syntax.

Static validation and type checking verify structural correctness; they cannot verify that the code does what the user intended. A function that compiles and passes its unit tests may still implement the wrong algorithm.
Latency.
The full pipeline — context retrieval, generation, validation, and possible retry — takes seconds to minutes depending on the size of the output and the number of retry cycles required.
Review burden on subtly incorrect outputs.
Generated code that is structurally correct but logically wrong is harder to review than obviously broken code. Engineers must read generated code with as much care as any other change.
How It Works

The code generation pipeline has four distinct phases. Each phase can fail independently, and the pipeline must handle failure at each stage by either correcting locally or looping back to an earlier stage.
Phase 1: context retrieval

Before generating a single token of code, the agent retrieves the structural facts the LLM will need: the signatures of every function the new code will call, the types of every value it will handle, the interfaces it must satisfy, and representative examples of similar code already in the codebase. Retrieval is the most critical phase for correctness — a generation with excellent context but imperfect LLM output can be repaired in the validation phase, but a generation with missing or wrong context will produce output that passes local checks but fails at the system level.
Phase 2: prompt assembly

The prompt is a carefully ordered concatenation of the task description, the retrieved context, and one or more few-shot examples. The ordering matters: placing the most relevant context — the function signatures the new code must conform to, the interface it must implement — immediately before the generation target reduces the distance between the context tokens and the output tokens, which improves adherence in practice. Stop sequences are set to terminate generation at the end of the syntactic unit being produced (a closing brace, a blank line after a function body) to prevent over-generation.
Phase 3: generation strategies

Three generation strategies are common in production coding agents. Greedy decoding (temperature 0) produces deterministic, maximally probable output and is preferred when the task is well-specified and the context is complete — typical for boilerplate generation. Low-temperature sampling (0.1–0.3) introduces slight variation and is useful for tasks where multiple plausible implementations exist. Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM) is a model capability that allows generating code to be inserted between a prefix and a suffix already provided — it is the correct strategy for inserting a function body into an existing file, because it can see the surrounding code on both sides.

Multi-turn refinement treats generation as a conversation: the initial output is evaluated, the errors or gaps are identified, and a follow-up prompt asks the model to fix them. This is the fallback when a single-pass generation fails validation repeatedly. Speculative decoding (using a small draft model to propose tokens verified by a larger judge model) can reduce latency for long-form generation without reducing quality.
Phase 4: validation and retry

Generated output is validated before being written to the filesystem. The validation stack typically runs in order: (1) parse the output to confirm it is syntactically valid for the target language; (2) apply the type checker to verify types against the imported signatures; (3) run the linter to check style conformance; (4) run the relevant unit tests to verify behavioral correctness. Each check produces structured feedback (error type, line number, message) that is injected into the retry prompt if validation fails. The retry loop typically runs up to three times before the agent escalates to asking for human guidance.
Architecture Overview

The diagram above shows the complete pipeline. A task request enters from the left and flows through context retrieval (which draws from the codebase knowledge store), prompt assembly, and LLM generation. The output passes through static validation and test execution before becoming finalized generated code. The dashed red arc from static validation back to the LLM represents the retry path: validation errors are formatted and appended to the prompt, and the model re-generates.

The codebase knowledge store is populated ahead of the generation call. It contains a symbol index (function definitions and call sites), a type graph (resolved types and signatures from LSP), a vector embedding store (for semantic example retrieval), few-shot code patterns (examples of similar constructs already in the codebase), and ancillary stores for style guides, test fixtures, the dependency graph, and git history. The generation strategies panel on the right shows the four options the agent selects from based on task characteristics.
When to Use It

Code generation is appropriate when the target code is derivable from a specification the agent can access — a type interface, a schema, a set of existing analogous implementations, or a natural language description precise enough to constrain the output. The more of these inputs are available, the more reliable the generation.

Generation is particularly effective for: implementing a function body given its full type signature and docstring; generating a test suite given an existing implementation; producing a serializer or deserializer from a data model; writing a migration script from a schema diff; and scaffolding a new module that follows a pattern established by existing modules in the same directory.

Generation is less appropriate when the task requires deep domain knowledge not present in the codebase (novel algorithms, proprietary business logic the agent cannot infer), when the output must be optimal rather than merely correct (performance-critical inner loops), or when the specification is deliberately vague and the user needs to explore the design space rather than implement a fixed solution.
How to Implement

A practical code generation tool for a Python coding agent needs three components: a context builder that retrieves signatures and examples, a prompt assembler, and a validation harness. The following shows each component in isolation, then wired together.
Context builder

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from pathlib import Path
import subprocess, json

@dataclass
class GenerationContext:
    task_description: str
    target_file: str
    target_function: str | None
    called_signatures: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    implemented_interface: str | None = None
    few_shot_examples: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    style_notes: str = ""


def gather_context(
    task: str,
    target_file: str,
    module_symbols: list[str],
    repo_root: Path,
) -> GenerationContext:
    """
    Build a GenerationContext by importing each symbol and retrieving
    its signature, plus finding similar code via keyword grep.
    """
    ctx = GenerationContext(
        task_description=task,
        target_file=target_file,
        target_function=None,
    )

    # Resolve each symbol's type signature using Python's inspect module
    for symbol in module_symbols:
        mod_name, attr = symbol.rsplit(".", 1)
        result = subprocess.run(
            ["python", "-c",
             f"import inspect, importlib; "
             f"m = importlib.import_module('{mod_name}'); "
             f"fn = getattr(m, '{attr}'); "
             f"print(inspect.signature(fn))"],
            capture_output=True, text=True, cwd=repo_root
        )
        if result.returncode == 0:
            ctx.called_signatures.append(f"{symbol}{result.stdout.strip()}")

    # Find similar existing functions via ripgrep keyword search
    # (replace with vector search in production for better recall)
    keywords = [w for w in task.lower().split() if len(w) > 4]
    if keywords:
        rg = subprocess.run(
            ["rg", "--json", "-l", "|".join(keywords[:3]), str(repo_root)],
            capture_output=True, text=True
        )
        for line in rg.stdout.splitlines()[:3]:
            obj = json.loads(line)
            if obj.get("type") == "match":
                path = obj["data"]["path"]["text"]
                snippet = Path(path).read_text()[:600]
                ctx.few_shot_examples.append(f"# From {path}:\n{snippet}")

    return ctx
Prompt assembler and generation loop

import anthropic

def assemble_prompt(ctx: GenerationContext) -> str:
    parts: list[str] = []
    parts.append(
        "You are a coding assistant. Generate the requested Python function.\n"
        "Respond with ONLY the function definition — no explanation, no markdown fences."
    )
    if ctx.called_signatures:
        parts.append("# Available APIs (do not redefine these):")
        for sig in ctx.called_signatures:
            parts.append(f"  {sig}")
    if ctx.implemented_interface:
        parts.append(f"# Interface to implement:\n{ctx.implemented_interface}")
    if ctx.few_shot_examples:
        parts.append("# Style examples from this codebase:")
        for ex in ctx.few_shot_examples[:2]:
            parts.append(ex)
    parts.append(f"# Task: {ctx.task_description}")
    parts.append(f"# File: {ctx.target_file}")
    parts.append("# Generate the function below:")
    return "\n".join(parts)


def generate_with_retry(
    ctx: GenerationContext,
    project_root: Path,
    max_retries: int = 3,
) -> str:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    prompt = assemble_prompt(ctx)
    code = ""

    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        message = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=2048,
            temperature=0 if attempt == 0 else 0.2,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
            stop_sequences=["\n\n\n"],
        )
        code = message.content[0].text.strip()

        # Strip accidental markdown fences
        if code.startswith("```"):
            lines = code.splitlines()
            code = "\n".join(lines[1:-1] if lines[-1] == "```" else lines[1:])

        result = validate_python(code, project_root)
        if result.passed:
            return code

        error_text = "\n".join(result.errors[:10])
        prompt = (
            f"{prompt}\n\n"
            f"The previous attempt failed validation with these errors:\n"
            f"{error_text}\n\n"
            f"Fix all errors and regenerate the complete function:"
        )

    return code  # best-effort after exhausting retries
Examples
Example 1 — validation harness
A validation harness that runs parse, typecheck, and lint checks on generated code, returning structured errors for the retry loop.

import ast, subprocess, tempfile
from pathlib import Path
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ValidationResult:
    passed: bool
    errors: list[str]


def validate_python(code: str, project_root: Path) -> ValidationResult:
    """
    Run three checks on a generated Python snippet:
    1. AST parse — syntax check
    2. pyright — type check (via temp file)
    3. ruff — lint check (via stdin)
    Returns all errors found across all checks.
    """
    errors: list[str] = []

    # 1. Syntax check — cheapest, run first
    try:
        ast.parse(code)
    except SyntaxError as e:
        errors.append(f"SyntaxError at line {e.lineno}: {e.msg}")
        return ValidationResult(passed=False, errors=errors)

    # 2. Type check via pyright --outputjson
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(
        suffix=".py", dir=project_root, delete=False, mode="w"
    ) as f:
        tmp = Path(f.name)
        f.write(code)
    try:
        import json as _json
        result = subprocess.run(
            ["pyright", "--outputjson", str(tmp)],
            capture_output=True, text=True, cwd=project_root
        )
        data = _json.loads(result.stdout)
        for diag in data.get("generalDiagnostics", []):
            if diag.get("severity") == "error":
                line = diag["range"]["start"]["line"] + 1
                errors.append(f"TypeError line {line}: {diag['message']}")
    except Exception as exc:
        errors.append(f"pyright unavailable: {exc}")
    finally:
        tmp.unlink(missing_ok=True)

    # 3. ruff lint via stdin
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["ruff", "check", "--select=E,F", "-"],
        input=code, capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        for line in result.stdout.splitlines()[:5]:
            errors.append(f"Lint: {line}")

    return ValidationResult(passed=len(errors) == 0, errors=errors)


# Usage example
from pathlib import Path
result = validate_python(
    'def add(a: int, b: int) -> int:\n    return a + b\n',
    project_root=Path(".")
)
print(result.passed, result.errors)  # True, []
Example 2 — Fill-in-the-Middle insertion
Using Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM) format to insert a function body into an existing file while respecting the code that follows.

def generate_fim(
    prefix: str,
    suffix: str,
    task_description: str,
    model: str = "claude-opus-4-5",
) -> str:
    """
    Generate code to fill between  and  using Fill-in-the-Middle framing.
    This prevents the model from redefining names that appear in the suffix.

    prefix: everything in the file before the insertion point
    suffix: everything in the file after the insertion point
    """
    import anthropic
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    # FIM prompt format: prefix + task instruction + suffix context
    prompt = (
        f"# Existing file content before insertion point:\n{prefix}\n\n"
        f"# Task: {task_description}\n"
        f"# The following code comes AFTER the insertion point (do not regenerate it):\n"
        f"# {suffix[:300]}\n\n"
        f"# Generate ONLY the code to insert between prefix and suffix.\n"
        f"# Do not duplicate anything already in prefix or suffix.\n"
    )

    message = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=1024,
        temperature=0,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        stop_sequences=["# The following code"],  # stop before re-emitting suffix
    )
    return message.content[0].text.strip()


# Example: insert a method into an existing class
prefix = '''class UserRepository:
    def __init__(self, db):
        self.db = db

    def get_by_id(self, user_id: int):
        return self.db.query(User).filter(User.id == user_id).first()

'''
suffix = '''    def delete(self, user_id: int) -> None:
        user = self.get_by_id(user_id)
        if user:
            self.db.delete(user)
            self.db.commit()
'''

new_method = generate_fim(
    prefix=prefix,
    suffix=suffix,
    task_description="Add a method that lists all users with pagination support",
)
print(new_method)
Real-World Example

Consider a coding agent tasked with adding a new endpoint GET /users/{id}/permissions to a Flask REST API. The codebase has 40 existing endpoints across 8 route files, all following a consistent pattern.
Pattern discovery.

The agent uses embedding search to find the three most similar existing endpoints (other user-related GET routes). It reads the full implementation of each to extract the pattern: route decorator, auth check, service call, response serialization.
Signature resolution.

LSP hover on the existing service layer shows that UserService.get_permissions(user_id: UUID) -> list[Permission] already exists. The agent reads the Permission type definition to understand what fields must be serialized.
Prompt assembly.

The prompt contains: the three similar route implementations as few-shot examples, the UserService.get_permissions signature, the Permission model definition, and the task description. Total prompt: approximately 800 tokens.
First-pass generation.

The model produces a route function at temperature 0. The function matches the existing pattern exactly: Flask route decorator, @require_auth wrapper, service call, jsonify response with the correct field names.
Validation passes first time.
Parse: clean. Pyright: zero errors. Ruff: one line-length warning, auto-fixed. A test for the new endpoint (generated in a second call) also passes.

Total elapsed time: 23 seconds. The result is indistinguishable in style from the hand-written endpoints because the generation was grounded in three concrete examples from the same file, not in a generic description of Flask routing.
Common Mistakes
Generating without retrieved signatures

The most common failure mode is omitting the signature retrieval step and relying on the LLM's training knowledge for API shapes. This produces code that compiles but calls functions with incorrect argument order, wrong types, or slightly misspelled names. The fix is to retrieve the actual current signature from the running codebase before generating.

# Wrong: relying on training knowledge
prompt = """
Task: Call save_record to persist the user.
Generate a function that does this.
"""
# The model may generate save_record(data) when the actual signature is
# save_record(record: UserRecord, *, overwrite: bool = False) -> SaveResult

# Right: retrieve the real signature first
import inspect, importlib
mod = importlib.import_module("src.storage.records")
sig = str(inspect.signature(mod.save_record))
# sig = '(record: UserRecord, *, overwrite: bool = False) -> SaveResult'
prompt = f"""
Task: Call save_record to persist the user.
Available API: save_record{sig}
Generate a function that does this.
"""
Using temperature > 0 for well-specified tasks

When the task is fully specified — a function with a known signature implementing a known interface — temperature 0 produces the most correct output. Higher temperature introduces variation that deviates from specified types or introduces unnecessary complexity. Reserve sampling for tasks where multiple valid implementations exist.
Not capping output length with stop sequences

Without an appropriate max_tokens limit and stop sequences, models over-generate: producing a function followed by an explanation, additional helper functions, or unrequested unit tests. Set max_tokens to 1.5–2x the expected output size and use stop sequences to terminate at the natural end of the syntactic unit.
Best Practices
Always use Fill-in-the-Middle when inserting into an existing file.
FIM gives the model visibility into the code immediately after the insertion point, preventing it from redefining names already declared below.
Include the existing file's import block in the prompt.
The imports at the top of the target file tell the model which names are already in scope, preventing duplicate imports or imports from a different path than the project convention.
Separate generation from insertion.
Generate code into a string, validate it, then write to disk. Never write partially-validated output to the target file. Use a temp file for type-check runs to avoid corrupting the working tree.
Run the relevant test subset after generation.
Type checking confirms structural correctness; tests confirm behavioral correctness. Identify which test file covers the modified module and run it as the final validation step.
Log generation metadata alongside the output.
Record which model, prompt version, temperature, and context sources were used. This enables post-hoc analysis of which configurations produce the highest first-pass validation rates.
Limit few-shot examples to the same file or module.

Examples drawn from the same module as the generation target produce better style adherence than examples from elsewhere in the codebase. Proximity in file structure correlates with proximity in idiom and abstraction level.
Performance & Scalability

The dominant latency contributor in the generation pipeline is the LLM call itself. For a 500-token prompt generating a 200-token function, a hosted frontier model returns in 1–3 seconds. For a 2,000-token prompt generating a 1,000-token output, the same model takes 8–15 seconds. Latency scales with output length (generation is sequential) more steeply than with prompt length (prompts are processed in parallel).

Validation adds fixed overhead. A Python syntax parse is under 1 ms. A pyright type check on a single file takes 500 ms to 2 seconds depending on the project's dependency graph. Running the test suite for a single module ranges from under a second for isolated unit tests to tens of seconds for integration tests. Structuring the validation stack so that cheap checks run first (parse before typecheck, typecheck before tests) minimizes wasted time on outputs that fail early.

For agents generating large outputs (complete files, complex classes), breaking the generation into multiple smaller calls — one per function — is more reliable than attempting the entire file in one call. Each smaller generation has more context budget available, validation errors are localized, and retries are cheaper. Final assembly of validated fragments is a deterministic operation that requires no model call.

Context caching dramatically reduces cost for repeated generation tasks on the same codebase. The prefix of the prompt containing the project's type stubs, interface definitions, and style guide changes infrequently. Caching this prefix with the model provider eliminates re-processing those tokens on every call, reducing both latency and cost by 60–80% for typical workloads.

Security & Reliability

Generated code inherits the security properties of its context. If the few-shot examples in the prompt use raw SQL string concatenation, the generated code will too. If the examples use parameterized queries, the generated code will follow suit. This means the quality of the few-shot examples is a security control: curating examples that follow secure coding practices (parameterized queries, proper input validation, safe deserialization) propagates those practices into all generated output without requiring the model to reason about security independently.

The validation stack must include a security-focused lint pass for any generated code that handles external input or performs privileged operations. Tools like Bandit (Python) and Semgrep (multi-language) can be added to the validation chain to flag known-dangerous patterns — shell injection via subprocess.run(shell=True), use of eval, hardcoded credentials — before the code is written to the filesystem. Failing these checks should trigger a retry with an explicit instruction to avoid the detected pattern.

Reliability under LLM failure requires handling the case where the model produces output that fails all retry attempts. The agent must not write this output to the codebase silently. The correct behavior is to write the best-attempt output to a temporary staging area, annotate it with the validation errors, and surface it to a human for review rather than either discarding it (losing the work) or committing it (corrupting the codebase).

Prompt injection is a relevant concern when user-supplied text appears in the generation prompt. Separating the system prompt (which includes all retrieved context and structural instructions) from the user-supplied task description, and limiting what the task description is allowed to reference, reduces but does not eliminate this risk.
Key Takeaways
Code generation is a pipeline, not a single LLM call. Context retrieval, prompt assembly, generation, and validation are all required steps for reliable output.
Retrieved signatures are the most important context. Knowing the exact type signatures of every function the generated code calls eliminates the largest class of generation errors.
Few-shot examples from the same file produce better style adherence than generic examples. Proximity in the codebase correlates with proximity in idiom and abstraction level.
Fill-in-the-Middle mode is the correct strategy for inserting code into an existing file. It gives the model visibility into the code immediately following the insertion point.
Validation must run in order of cost: parse first, then typecheck, then lint, then tests. Early failures save the expense of later checks on fundamentally broken output.

Security properties propagate from examples. Curating few-shot examples that follow secure coding practices is a more reliable security control than hoping the model reasons about security independently.

Context caching reduces repeated generation cost by 60–80% when the same type stubs and interface definitions appear in many prompts. Structure prompts with stable content first to maximize cache hits.
When all retries fail, stage the best-attempt output for human review rather than discarding it or silently committing it. Partial success is valuable signal, not a binary failure.
